name: Biology
description: Cells, life sciences, and living organisms

questions.q: What is the basic structural and functional unit of life?

source.notes: Discovered by Robert Hooke in 1665. Cell theory: all living things are made of cells.

questions.q: Which cellular process do plants use to convert sunlight into energy?

source.notes: Takes place in chloroplasts. Produces glucose and oxygen from CO₂ and water.

questions.q: What extinction event is believed to have wiped out the dinosaurs?

source.notes: About 66 million years ago. Chicxulub crater in Mexico is the impact site.

questions.q: A baby fish is commonly called what?

source.notes: Very young fish. 'Fingerlings' are slightly older juveniles.

questions.q: How many lungs does a typical mammal have?

source.notes: Except certain species of whales, which still have two but often only use one at a time.

questions.q: What is the name of the molecule that carries genetic information?

source.notes: Deoxyribonucleic acid. Double helix structure discovered by Watson, Crick, Franklin in 1953.

questions.q: What pain-relieving drug, originally derived from willow bark, became widely commercialized in the 1890s?

source.notes: Acetylsalicylic acid. Synthesized by Felix Hoffmann at Bayer in 1897.

questions.q: Belladonna, a poisonous plant, has a name that translates from Italian as what?

source.notes: Also called deadly nightshade. Contains atropine. Was historically used by women to dilate pupils.

questions.q: What is the medical name for 'athlete's foot'?

source.notes: Fungal infection. 'Tinea' = fungal skin infection, 'pedis' = of the foot.

questions.q: Which sweet liquid do flowers produce to attract pollinators?

source.notes: Contains sugars and water. Bees convert it into honey through regurgitation and evaporation.

questions.q: What is the largest organ in the human body?

source.notes: Average adult has ~20 sq ft of skin, weighing ~8 lbs.

questions.q: What is the powerhouse of the cell, responsible for producing ATP?

source.notes: Famous line from textbooks. Has its own DNA — thought to be originally a separate bacterium.

questions.q: What is the study of heredity and genes called?

source.notes: Term coined by William Bateson in 1905. Mendel is considered the founding father.

questions.q: Who developed the theory of evolution by natural selection?

source.notes: 'On the Origin of Species' published 1859. Co-developed with Alfred Russel Wallace.

questions.q: What percentage of the human body is water?

source.notes: Adults: about 55-60%. Infants: ~78%. Varies with body fat percentage.

questions.q: What blood type is considered the 'universal donor' for red blood cells?

source.notes: O-negative has no A, B, or Rh antigens. AB+ is the universal recipient.

questions.q: How many chambers does the human heart have?

source.notes: Two atria (upper) and two ventricles (lower). Fish have 2 chambers, amphibians 3.

questions.q: Amphibians typically lay their eggs in what kind of environment?

source.notes: Frogs, toads, salamanders. Eggs lack shells and would dry out on land.

questions.q: What is the scientific term for the process of cell division that produces sex cells (gametes)?

source.notes: Produces 4 haploid cells from 1 diploid parent. Mitosis makes identical diploid cells.

questions.q: Who is often called the 'Father of Modern Genetics' for his work with pea plants?

source.notes: Austrian monk. Published his 'laws of inheritance' in 1866, largely ignored until rediscovered in 1900.
